1: <header>Energy use and greenhouse gas emissions in organic and conventional grain crop production: Accounting for nutrient inflows </header>
Eric Hoffman Michel A. Cavigellib,*, Gustavo Camargoc, Matthew Ryand, Victoria J. Ackroyd , Tom L. Richard Steven Mirsky

<list>a School of Public Policy, University of Maryland, College Park, MD 20742, USA 
b Sustainable Agricultural Systems Lab, USDA-ARS, Beltsville, MD 20705, USA 
c Department of Agricultural and Biological Engineering, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, PA 16802, USA 
d Section of Soil and Crop Sciences, School of Integrative Plant Sciences, Cornell University, Ithaca, NY 14850, USA 
e Department of Plant Science and Landscape Architecture, University of Maryland, College Park, MD 20742, USA</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Energy use Greenhouse gas emissions Life cycle analysis Cover crops Crop rotation|Agriculture is a large source of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and has large energy requirements. Previous research has shown that organic farming and conservation tillage practices can reduce environmental impacts from agriculture. We used the Farm Energy Analysis Tool (FEAT) to quantify the energy use and GHG emissions on area (ha) and crop yield (kg crop) bases for five cropping systems that comprise the Farming Systems Project (FSP) at the USDA-Agricultural Research Service (ARS), Beltsville Agricultural Research Center in Maryland, US. The FSP consists of five grain cropping systems that mimic those used in the mid-Atlantic region of the US: 1) a 3- year conventional no-till corn (Zea mays L.)-soybean (Glycine max (L.) Merr)-wheat (Triticum aestivum L.)/ soybean rotation (NT), 2) a 3-year conventional chisel-till corn-soybean-wheat/soybean rotation (CT), 3) a 2- year organic corn-soybean rotation (Org2), 4) a 3-year organic corn-soybean-wheat rotation (Org3), and 5) a 6- year organic corn-soybean-wheat-alfalfa (Medicago sativa L.) rotation (Org6). We accounted for nutrient inflows into organic systems by using a mass-energy allocation method, which accounts for the total energy and GHG emissions from the original production of nutrients found in poultry litter through synthetic fertilizer production (N) and nutrient mining (P and K). We believe this is the first attempt to quantify energy use and GHG emissions from nutrients applied in organic systems that originated through industrial processes used in conventional agriculture. Energy use was greatest in the conventional systems when expressed on a per area basis, with energy costs of producing synthetic N fertilizer accounting for 45 to 46% of total energy use. When expressed per unit of crop yield, energy use was greatest in Org2, lowest in Org6, and similar in Org3, NT and CT. Energy use de- creased with increasing crop rotation length and complexity among organic systems whether expressed on an area or yield basis. Greenhouse gas emissions were higher in the Org2 and Org3 systems than in the conventional systems and were lowest in Org6 whether expressed on an area or yield basis. Our results indicate that organic management consistently had lower energy use than conventional management on an area basis, but not when expressed on a crop yield basis. Of particular interest is that diversifying grain cropping systems to include perennials was a more effective management strategy than organic management per se to reduce energy use and GHG emissions in agriculture.
2: Increasing energy efficiency and reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions to mitigate climate change are two important goals for agriculture (USDA, 2015). In 2015, US agriculture was responsible for 6587 teragrams of CO2 equivalents (COe.), or 9% of total US GHG emissions, mostly as methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) (USDA ERS, 2016). Since the EPA accounting method does not allocate COe

for the production and transport of agricultural inputs to agriculture but to industrial and transportation sectors, respectively, the impact of agriculture is higher when these products are allocated to the sector in which they are applied. On-farm energy use in the US totaled 0.84 exajoule in 2008, about 0.8% of total energy use in the US (USDA, 2011). Direct energy use (i.e. fuel to operate machinery for field op- erations) accounted for 63% of farm energy use, with the remaining 37% resulting from production of inputs such as fertilizers and

herbicides (Beckman et al., 2013). Fertilizer production, particularly nitrogen (N), is energy intensive and is also an important source of GHG emissions (Wood et al., 2004). Several studies estimate that N fertilizer production alone accounts for 1.2% of total worldwide energy use and GHG emissions (Kongshaug, 1998; Swaminathan, 2004) and, along with P and K fertilizers, up to 29% of total energy use in the US agri- culture sector (Schnepf, 2004). Management practices and cropping systems that reduce or eliminate synthetic fertilizer inputs could po- tentially reduce GHG emissions and energy use in agriculture, but holistic assessments of diverse cropping systems are needed to under- stand tradeoffs between management practices and environmental im- pacts.

Since organic farming systems prohibit the use of synthetic fertili- zers they are often identified as means of decreasing energy use and GHG emissions in agriculture. For example, Smith et al. (2015) ana- lyzed data from nearly fifty studies and found that organic farming systems consistently use less energy than conventional systems when expressed on a unit of area basis. Results vary when expressed on a unit of crop basis due to lower yields for most organic crops. Gomiero et al. (2008) conducted a meta-analysis of comparisons between organic and conventional farming systems and found that organic systems required 10-70% less energy on a unit of land basis and 15-45% less on a unit of product basis. Greenhouse gas emissions from organic systems were also lower, by 32 to 71%, compared to those from conventional systems on a unit of land basis. Production of several organic grain crops, in- cluding winter wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) in Europe (Küstermann et al., 2008) and certain fruit and vegetable crops (e.g., apples (Malus pumila Mill.) and potatoes (Solanum tuberosum L.)) (Pimentel et al., 1983), were found to require more energy or emit higher levels of GHGs than conventional counterparts when compared on a unit of product basis, largely due to relatively lower yields in these organic systems.

Energy use and GHG emission contributions from organic farming systems are also dependent in part on the use of manure from con- ventionally raised animals in place of synthetic fertilizer. One important consideration when evaluating the environmental impact of animal manure is how to allocate the environmental burdens from a product (e.g. broiler meat) and its co-products (e.g. poultry litter). The alloca- tion method used to assess the environmental impact of product and co- product can influence results. For example, Casey and Holden (2005) reported that in milk production, allocation based on product and co- product weight led to the attribution of higher GHG emissions than the economic allocation method, which allocates environmental impacts based on the economic value of a product and its co-products.

A review of livestock production assessments found that economic allocation was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as manure (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its po- pularity in the literature, economic allocation has been the subject of recent criticism. First, allocation decisions are driven by market forces, which often externalize environmental impacts of production systems, and therefore do not accurately capture the full environmental impacts of a production system. Additionally, the division of impacts can change based on price changes in the market (Pelletier and Tyedmers, 2011). For example, if demand for poultry litter were to rise due to increased costs of synthetic fertilizers or more farmland shifting to organic pro- duction in a region, then an increasingly higher share of the environ- mental impact would be attributed to poultry litter, even if there was no change in poultry litter production and the amount applied to soils. Pelletier (2008) reported that litter management accounts for 9.7% of the energy used to produce chickens but only 1.2% of the GHG emis- sions. Economic allocation would attribute one constant value for both the energy needs and GHG emissions to litter management despite differences in impact intensities. Capturing the specific environmental impact of the litter is difficult when those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. Finally, economic allocation assumes that poultry litter is always a co-product of poultry production with an economic value; but, depending on geographic and temporal

factors the same litter could be considered a by-product or even a waste with little to no economic value. In Maryland, poultry litter is unevenly distributed geographically and the co-product (i.e. poultry litter) can have fertilizer value that exceeds handling costs in one location while the handling costs are greater than the fertilizer value in another lo- cation. Such factors make it difficult to assign one constant dollar value to poultry litter, even within a single state.

The International Standards Organization (ISO) provides re- commendations on how to allocate co-products. ISO recommends system expansion whenever possible to avoid the need for co-product allocation. System expansion involves expanding the boundaries of an assessment to include indirect impacts of a production system outside the scope of the system in question (ISO, 1998). Using this method, poultry farms would be credited with the environmental benefit of avoided synthetic fertilizer production through the production of poultry litter for fertilizing crops. When system expansion is not rea- listic and allocation of environmental impacts cannot be avoided, the ISO recommends that allocation reflect physical relationships between a process and its environmental impacts, or some other relationship between products and co-products. Economic allocation is reserved for when other allocation methods are not possible or realistic (ISO, 1998).

The mass-energy approach allocates environmental burdens from litter based on a biophysical relationship between poultry litter and the inputs needed for its production. These inputs include industrially produced or processed fertilizers that were applied in conventional cropping systems to grow feed for poultry. Most of the nutrients found in poultry litter, therefore, originated from conventional production systems. This mass-energy methodology follows the ISO 14044 re- commendation that the "inventory is based on material balances be- tween inputs and outputs. Allocation procedures should therefore ap- proximate as much as possible such fundamental input/output relationships and characteristics" (ISO, 2006). Such a method is useful when quantifying the dependence of organic farming systems on con- ventional production processes since the use of litter is tied directly to the production of nutrients in the litter.

In this paper, we present two allocation methods. We used economic allocation, which assigns values for energy consumption and GHG emissions from poultry litter production based on the economic value of poultry litter, since it has been the most common allocation method used in previous livestock life cycle analyses (LCAs) and therefore al- lows our results to be compared with previous studies (Thomassen et al., 2008). This method is also appealing since poultry are raised for their meat or eggs, and litter is a co-product that would be produced whether or not it is applied to farms. Therefore, it can be argued that litter should only be charged a proportion of the environmental impacts from poultry production based on the relatively small economic value compared to poultry meat.

Recognizing the limits of economic allocation mentioned above, we also conducted allocation on a mass-energy basis. This method reflects the flow of biologically valuable resources (N, P, and K) and their en- vironmental impacts. The benefit of this methodology is that it directly reflects the relationship between poultry litter and the resources needed to produce that litter. Feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental impact of poultry production (Boggia et al., 2010), and this feed is typically grown in conventional cropping systems that de- pend on synthetic fertilizer, synthetic herbicides and pesticides. Taking into account the inputs needed to produce poultry litter and the nu- trients found in the litter may better reflect the true environmental costs of using poultry litter in both organic and conventional cropping sys- tems.

We chose not to use system expansion since our goal was to allocate the cost of manure nutrients to the organic systems to recognize their dependence on synthetic nutrients and the associated environmental impacts. Additionally, due to the uneven geographic distribution of poultry litter in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, transportation of poultry litter from areas of production to farms across the state is often

subsidized to limit nutrient runoff into the Chesapeake Bay (http:// ida.maryland.gov/resource_conservation/Pages/manure_ management.aspx https://dda.delaware.gov/nutrients/nm_reloc. shtml). System expansion would credit the poultry farm for displacing the production of synthetic fertilizer but would ignore the fact that excess poultry litter carries other environmental burdens. If these nu- trients are not recycled as fertilizer they can end up in the Chesapeake Bay, which can contribute to harmful algal blooms and other environ- mental problems. Given this situation, it is important to track the en- vironmental burden of the specific nutrients in the poultry litter rather than assuming poultry production in general produces only environ- mental benefits through synthetic fertilizer displacement in the mar- ketplace.

The primary goal of this study was to quantify GHG emissions and energy use in organic systems, including those associated with the nutrients that originated through industrial processes used in conven- tional agriculture. We conducted an analysis of energy use and GHG emissions in five different cropping systems in the FSP, a long-term grain cropping systems field experiment which contains both organic and conventional treatments (Cavigelli et al., 2008). We ultimately sought to identify which inputs and management decisions drove dif- ferences in energy use and GHG emissions. The scope of our analysis was limited to the farm gate.

Several studies estimate that N fertilizer production alone accounts for 1.2% of total worldwide energy use and GHG emissions (Kongshaug, 1998; Swaminathan, 2004) and, along with P and K fertilizers, up to 29% of total energy use in the US agri- culture sector (Schnepf, 2004). Management practices and cropping systems that reduce or eliminate synthetic fertilizer inputs could po- tentially reduce GHG emissions and energy use in agriculture, but holistic assessments of diverse cropping systems are needed to under- stand tradeoffs between management practices and environmental im- pacts.

Since organic farming systems prohibit the use of synthetic fertili- zers they are often identified as means of decreasing energy use and GHG emissions in agriculture. For example, Smith et al. (2015) ana- lyzed data from nearly fifty studies and found that organic farming systems consistently use less energy than conventional systems when expressed on a unit of area basis. Results vary when expressed on a unit of crop basis due to lower yields for most organic crops. Gomiero et al. (2008) conducted a meta-analysis of comparisons between organic and conventional farming systems and found that organic systems required 10-70% less energy on a unit of land basis and 15-45% less on a unit of product basis. Greenhouse gas emissions from organic systems were also lower, by 32 to 71%, compared to those from conventional systems on a unit of land basis. Production of several organic grain crops, in- cluding winter wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) in Europe (Küstermann et al., 2008) and certain fruit and vegetable crops (e.g., apples (Malus pumila Mill.) and potatoes (Solanum tuberosum L.)) (Pimentel et al., 1983), were found to require more energy or emit higher levels of GHGs than conventional counterparts when compared on a unit of product basis, largely due to relatively lower yields in these organic systems.

Energy use and GHG emission contributions from organic farming systems are also dependent in part on the use of manure from con- ventionally raised animals in place of synthetic fertilizer. One important consideration when evaluating the environmental impact of animal manure is how to allocate the environmental burdens from a product (e.g. broiler meat) and its co-products (e.g. poultry litter). The alloca- tion method used to assess the environmental impact of product and co- product can influence results. For example, Casey and Holden (2005) reported that in milk production, allocation based on product and co- product weight led to the attribution of higher GHG emissions than the economic allocation method, which allocates environmental impacts based on the economic value of a product and its co-products.

A review of livestock production assessments found that economic allocation was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as manure (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its po- pularity in the literature, economic allocation has been the subject of recent criticism. First, allocation decisions are driven by market forces, which often externalize environmental impacts of production systems, and therefore do not accurately capture the full environmental impacts of a production system. Additionally, the division of impacts can change based on price changes in the market (Pelletier and Tyedmers, 2011). For example, if demand for poultry litter were to rise due to increased costs of synthetic fertilizers or more farmland shifting to organic pro- duction in a region, then an increasingly higher share of the environ- mental impact would be attributed to poultry litter, even if there was no change in poultry litter production and the amount applied to soils. Pelletier (2008) reported that litter management accounts for 9.7% of the energy used to produce chickens but only 1.2% of the GHG emis- sions. Economic allocation would attribute one constant value for both the energy needs and GHG emissions to litter management despite differences in impact intensities. Capturing the specific environmental impact of the litter is difficult when those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. Finally, economic allocation assumes that poultry litter is always a co-product of poultry production with an economic value; but, depending on geographic and temporal

factors the same litter could be considered a by-product or even a waste with little to no economic value. In Maryland, poultry litter is unevenly distributed geographically and the co-product (i.e. poultry litter) can have fertilizer value that exceeds handling costs in one location while the handling costs are greater than the fertilizer value in another lo- cation. Such factors make it difficult to assign one constant dollar value to poultry litter, even within a single state.

The International Standards Organization (ISO) provides re- commendations on how to allocate co-products. ISO recommends system expansion whenever possible to avoid the need for co-product allocation. System expansion involves expanding the boundaries of an assessment to include indirect impacts of a production system outside the scope of the system in question (ISO, 1998). Using this method, poultry farms would be credited with the environmental benefit of avoided synthetic fertilizer production through the production of poultry litter for fertilizing crops. When system expansion is not rea- listic and allocation of environmental impacts cannot be avoided, the ISO recommends that allocation reflect physical relationships between a process and its environmental impacts, or some other relationship between products and co-products. Economic allocation is reserved for when other allocation methods are not possible or realistic (ISO, 1998).

The mass-energy approach allocates environmental burdens from litter based on a biophysical relationship between poultry litter and the inputs needed for its production. These inputs include industrially produced or processed fertilizers that were applied in conventional cropping systems to grow feed for poultry. Most of the nutrients found in poultry litter, therefore, originated from conventional production systems. This mass-energy methodology follows the ISO 14044 re- commendation that the "inventory is based on material balances be- tween inputs and outputs. Allocation procedures should therefore ap- proximate as much as possible such fundamental input/output relationships and characteristics" (ISO, 2006). Such a method is useful when quantifying the dependence of organic farming systems on con- ventional production processes since the use of litter is tied directly to the production of nutrients in the litter.

In this paper, we present two allocation methods. We used economic allocation, which assigns values for energy consumption and GHG emissions from poultry litter production based on the economic value of poultry litter, since it has been the most common allocation method used in previous livestock life cycle analyses (LCAs) and therefore al- lows our results to be compared with previous studies (Thomassen et al., 2008). This method is also appealing since poultry are raised for their meat or eggs, and litter is a co-product that would be produced whether or not it is applied to farms. Therefore, it can be argued that litter should only be charged a proportion of the environmental impacts from poultry production based on the relatively small economic value compared to poultry meat.

Recognizing the limits of economic allocation mentioned above, we also conducted allocation on a mass-energy basis. This method reflects the flow of biologically valuable resources (N, P, and K) and their en- vironmental impacts. The benefit of this methodology is that it directly reflects the relationship between poultry litter and the resources needed to produce that litter. Feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental impact of poultry production (Boggia et al., 2010), and this feed is typically grown in conventional cropping systems that de- pend on synthetic fertilizer, synthetic herbicides and pesticides. Taking into account the inputs needed to produce poultry litter and the nu- trients found in the litter may better reflect the true environmental costs of using poultry litter in both organic and conventional cropping sys- tems.

We chose not to use system expansion since our goal was to allocate the cost of manure nutrients to the organic systems to recognize their dependence on synthetic nutrients and the associated environmental impacts. Additionally, due to the uneven geographic distribution of poultry litter in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, transportation of poultry litter from areas of production to farms across the state is often

subsidized to limit nutrient runoff into the Chesapeake Bay (http:// ida.maryland.gov/resource_conservation/Pages/manure_ management.aspx https://dda.delaware.gov/nutrients/nm_reloc. shtml). System expansion would credit the poultry farm for displacing the production of synthetic fertilizer but would ignore the fact that excess poultry litter carries other environmental burdens. If these nu- trients are not recycled as fertilizer they can end up in the Chesapeake Bay, which can contribute to harmful algal blooms and other environ- mental problems. Given this situation, it is important to track the en- vironmental burden of the specific nutrients in the poultry litter rather than assuming poultry production in general produces only environ- mental benefits through synthetic fertilizer displacement in the mar- ketplace.

The primary goal of this study was to quantify GHG emissions and energy use in organic systems, including those associated with the nutrients that originated through industrial processes used in conven- tional agriculture. We conducted an analysis of energy use and GHG emissions in five different cropping systems in the FSP, a long-term grain cropping systems field experiment which contains both organic and conventional treatments (Cavigelli et al., 2008). We ultimately sought to identify which inputs and management decisions drove dif- ferences in energy use and GHG emissions. The scope of our analysis was limited to the farm gate.

Such factors make it difficult to assign one constant dollar value to poultry litter, even within a single state.

The International Standards Organization (ISO) provides re- commendations on how to allocate co-products. ISO recommends system expansion whenever possible to avoid the need for co-product allocation. System expansion involves expanding the boundaries of an assessment to include indirect impacts of a production system outside the scope of the system in question (ISO, 1998). Using this method, poultry farms would be credited with the environmental benefit of avoided synthetic fertilizer production through the production of poultry litter for fertilizing crops. When system expansion is not rea- listic and allocation of environmental impacts cannot be avoided, the ISO recommends that allocation reflect physical relationships between a process and its environmental impacts, or some other relationship between products and co-products. Economic allocation is reserved for when other allocation methods are not possible or realistic (ISO, 1998).

The mass-energy approach allocates environmental burdens from litter based on a biophysical relationship between poultry litter and the inputs needed for its production. These inputs include industrially produced or processed fertilizers that were applied in conventional cropping systems to grow feed for poultry. Most of the nutrients found in poultry litter, therefore, originated from conventional production systems. This mass-energy methodology follows the ISO 14044 re- commendation that the "inventory is based on material balances be- tween inputs and outputs. Allocation procedures should therefore ap- proximate as much as possible such fundamental input/output relationships and characteristics" (ISO, 2006). Such a method is useful when quantifying the dependence of organic farming systems on con- ventional production processes since the use of litter is tied directly to the production of nutrients in the litter.

In this paper, we present two allocation methods. We used economic allocation, which assigns values for energy consumption and GHG emissions from poultry litter production based on the economic value of poultry litter, since it has been the most common allocation method used in previous livestock life cycle analyses (LCAs) and therefore al- lows our results to be compared with previous studies (Thomassen et al., 2008). This method is also appealing since poultry are raised for their meat or eggs, and litter is a co-product that would be produced whether or not it is applied to farms. Therefore, it can be argued that litter should only be charged a proportion of the environmental impacts from poultry production based on the relatively small economic value compared to poultry meat.

Recognizing the limits of economic allocation mentioned above, we also conducted allocation on a mass-energy basis. This method reflects the flow of biologically valuable resources (N, P, and K) and their en- vironmental impacts. The benefit of this methodology is that it directly reflects the relationship between poultry litter and the resources needed to produce that litter. Feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental impact of poultry production (Boggia et al., 2010), and this feed is typically grown in conventional cropping systems that de- pend on synthetic fertilizer, synthetic herbicides and pesticides. Taking into account the inputs needed to produce poultry litter and the nu- trients found in the litter may better reflect the true environmental costs of using poultry litter in both organic and conventional cropping sys- tems.

We chose not to use system expansion since our goal was to allocate the cost of manure nutrients to the organic systems to recognize their dependence on synthetic nutrients and the associated environmental impacts. Additionally, due to the uneven geographic distribution of poultry litter in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, transportation of poultry litter from areas of production to farms across the state is often

subsidized to limit nutrient runoff into the Chesapeake Bay (http:// ida.maryland.gov/resource_conservation/Pages/manure_ management.aspx https://dda.delaware.gov/nutrients/nm_reloc. shtml). System expansion would credit the poultry farm for displacing the production of synthetic fertilizer but would ignore the fact that excess poultry litter carries other environmental burdens. If these nu- trients are not recycled as fertilizer they can end up in the Chesapeake Bay, which can contribute to harmful algal blooms and other environ- mental problems. Given this situation, it is important to track the en- vironmental burden of the specific nutrients in the poultry litter rather than assuming poultry production in general produces only environ- mental benefits through synthetic fertilizer displacement in the mar- ketplace.

The primary goal of this study was to quantify GHG emissions and energy use in organic systems, including those associated with the nutrients that originated through industrial processes used in conven- tional agriculture. We conducted an analysis of energy use and GHG emissions in five different cropping systems in the FSP, a long-term grain cropping systems field experiment which contains both organic and conventional treatments (Cavigelli et al., 2008). We ultimately sought to identify which inputs and management decisions drove dif- ferences in energy use and GHG emissions. The scope of our analysis was limited to the farm gate.
9: Increasing energy efficiency and reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions to mitigate climate change are two important goals for agriculture (USDA, 2015). In 2015, US agriculture was responsible for 6587 teragrams of CO2 equivalents (COe.), or 9% of total US GHG emissions, mostly as methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) (USDA ERS, 2016). Since the EPA accounting method does not allocate COe

for the production and transport of agricultural inputs to agriculture but to industrial and transportation sectors, respectively, the impact of agriculture is higher when these products are allocated to the sector in which they are applied. On-farm energy use in the US totaled 0.84 exajoule in 2008, about 0.8% of total energy use in the US (USDA, 2011). Direct energy use (i.e. fuel to operate machinery for field op- erations) accounted for 63% of farm energy use, with the remaining 37% resulting from production of inputs such as fertilizers and

herbicides (Beckman et al., 2013). Fertilizer production, particularly nitrogen (N), is energy intensive and is also an important source of GHG emissions (Wood et al., 2004). Several studies estimate that N fertilizer production alone accounts for 1.2% of total worldwide energy use and GHG emissions (Kongshaug, 1998; Swaminathan, 2004) and, along with P and K fertilizers, up to 29% of total energy use in the US agri- culture sector (Schnepf, 2004). Management practices and cropping systems that reduce or eliminate synthetic fertilizer inputs could po- tentially reduce GHG emissions and energy use in agriculture, but holistic assessments of diverse cropping systems are needed to under- stand tradeoffs between management practices and environmental im- pacts.

Since organic farming systems prohibit the use of synthetic fertili- zers they are often identified as means of decreasing energy use and GHG emissions in agriculture. For example, Smith et al. (2015) ana- lyzed data from nearly fifty studies and found that organic farming systems consistently use less energy than conventional systems when expressed on a unit of area basis. Results vary when expressed on a unit of crop basis due to lower yields for most organic crops. Gomiero et al. (2008) conducted a meta-analysis of comparisons between organic and conventional farming systems and found that organic systems required 10-70% less energy on a unit of land basis and 15-45% less on a unit of product basis. Greenhouse gas emissions from organic systems were also lower, by 32 to 71%, compared to those from conventional systems on a unit of land basis. Production of several organic grain crops, in- cluding winter wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) in Europe (Küstermann et al., 2008) and certain fruit and vegetable crops (e.g., apples (Malus pumila Mill.) and potatoes (Solanum tuberosum L.)) (Pimentel et al., 1983), were found to require more energy or emit higher levels of GHGs than conventional counterparts when compared on a unit of product basis, largely due to relatively lower yields in these organic systems.

Energy use and GHG emission contributions from organic farming systems are also dependent in part on the use of manure from con- ventionally raised animals in place of synthetic fertilizer. One important consideration when evaluating the environmental impact of animal manure is how to allocate the environmental burdens from a product (e.g. broiler meat) and its co-products (e.g. poultry litter). The alloca- tion method used to assess the environmental impact of product and co- product can influence results. For example, Casey and Holden (2005) reported that in milk production, allocation based on product and co- product weight led to the attribution of higher GHG emissions than the economic allocation method, which allocates environmental impacts based on the economic value of a product and its co-products.

A review of livestock production assessments found that economic allocation was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as manure (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its po- pularity in the literature, economic allocation has been the subject of recent criticism. First, allocation decisions are driven by market forces, which often externalize environmental impacts of production systems, and therefore do not accurately capture the full environmental impacts of a production system. Additionally, the division of impacts can change based on price changes in the market (Pelletier and Tyedmers, 2011). For example, if demand for poultry litter were to rise due to increased costs of synthetic fertilizers or more farmland shifting to organic pro- duction in a region, then an increasingly higher share of the environ- mental impact would be attributed to poultry litter, even if there was no change in poultry litter production and the amount applied to soils. Pelletier (2008) reported that litter management accounts for 9.7% of the energy used to produce chickens but only 1.2% of the GHG emis- sions. Economic allocation would attribute one constant value for both the energy needs and GHG emissions to litter management despite differences in impact intensities. Capturing the specific environmental impact of the litter is difficult when those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. Finally, economic allocation assumes that poultry litter is always a co-product of poultry production with an economic value; but, depending on geographic and temporal

factors the same litter could be considered a by-product or even a waste with little to no economic value. In Maryland, poultry litter is unevenly distributed geographically and the co-product (i.e. poultry litter) can have fertilizer value that exceeds handling costs in one location while the handling costs are greater than the fertilizer value in another lo- cation. Such factors make it difficult to assign one constant dollar value to poultry litter, even within a single state.

When system expansion is not rea- listic and allocation of environmental impacts cannot be avoided, the ISO recommends that allocation reflect physical relationships between a process and its environmental impacts, or some other relationship between products and co-products. Economic allocation is reserved for when other allocation methods are not possible or realistic (ISO, 1998).

We conducted an analysis of energy use and GHG emissions in five different cropping systems in the FSP, a long-term grain cropping systems field experiment which contains both organic and conventional treatments (Cavigelli et al., 2008). We ultimately sought to identify which inputs and management decisions drove dif- ferences in energy use and GHG emissions. The scope of our analysis was limited to the farm gate.
12: Increasing energy efficiency and reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions to mitigate climate change are two important goals for agriculture (USDA, 2015). In 2015, US agriculture was responsible for 6587 teragrams of CO2 equivalents (COe.), or 9% of total US GHG emissions, mostly as methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) (USDA ERS, 2016). Since the EPA accounting method does not allocate COe

for the production and transport of agricultural inputs to agriculture but to industrial and transportation sectors, respectively, the impact of agriculture is higher when these products are allocated to the sector in which they are applied. On-farm energy use in the US totaled 0.84 exajoule in 2008, about 0.8% of total energy use in the US (USDA, 2011). Direct energy use (i.e. fuel to operate machinery for field op- erations) accounted for 63% of farm energy use, with the remaining 37% resulting from production of inputs such as fertilizers and

herbicides (Beckman et al., 2013). Fertilizer production, particularly nitrogen (N), is energy intensive and is also an important source of GHG emissions (Wood et al., 2004). Several studies estimate that N fertilizer production alone accounts for 1.2% of total worldwide energy use and GHG emissions (Kongshaug, 1998; Swaminathan, 2004) and, along with P and K fertilizers, up to 29% of total energy use in the US agri- culture sector (Schnepf, 2004). Management practices and cropping systems that reduce or eliminate synthetic fertilizer inputs could po- tentially reduce GHG emissions and energy use in agriculture, but holistic assessments of diverse cropping systems are needed to under- stand tradeoffs between management practices and environmental im- pacts.

Since organic farming systems prohibit the use of synthetic fertili- zers they are often identified as means of decreasing energy use and GHG emissions in agriculture. For example, Smith et al. (2015) ana- lyzed data from nearly fifty studies and found that organic farming systems consistently use less energy than conventional systems when expressed on a unit of area basis. Results vary when expressed on a unit of crop basis due to lower yields for most organic crops. Gomiero et al. (2008) conducted a meta-analysis of comparisons between organic and conventional farming systems and found that organic systems required 10-70% less energy on a unit of land basis and 15-45% less on a unit of product basis. Greenhouse gas emissions from organic systems were also lower, by 32 to 71%, compared to those from conventional systems on a unit of land basis. Production of several organic grain crops, in- cluding winter wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) in Europe (Küstermann et al., 2008) and certain fruit and vegetable crops (e.g., apples (Malus pumila Mill.) and potatoes (Solanum tuberosum L.)) (Pimentel et al., 1983), were found to require more energy or emit higher levels of GHGs than conventional counterparts when compared on a unit of product basis, largely due to relatively lower yields in these organic systems.

Energy use and GHG emission contributions from organic farming systems are also dependent in part on the use of manure from con- ventionally raised animals in place of synthetic fertilizer. One important consideration when evaluating the environmental impact of animal manure is how to allocate the environmental burdens from a product (e.g. broiler meat) and its co-products (e.g. poultry litter). The alloca- tion method used to assess the environmental impact of product and co- product can influence results. For example, Casey and Holden (2005) reported that in milk production, allocation based on product and co- product weight led to the attribution of higher GHG emissions than the economic allocation method, which allocates environmental impacts based on the economic value of a product and its co-products.

A review of livestock production assessments found that economic allocation was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as manure (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its po- pularity in the literature, economic allocation has been the subject of recent criticism. First, allocation decisions are driven by market forces, which often externalize environmental impacts of production systems, and therefore do not accurately capture the full environmental impacts of a production system. Additionally, the division of impacts can change based on price changes in the market (Pelletier and Tyedmers, 2011). For example, if demand for poultry litter were to rise due to increased costs of synthetic fertilizers or more farmland shifting to organic pro- duction in a region, then an increasingly higher share of the environ- mental impact would be attributed to poultry litter, even if there was no change in poultry litter production and the amount applied to soils. Pelletier (2008) reported that litter management accounts for 9.7% of the energy used to produce chickens but only 1.2% of the GHG emis- sions. Economic allocation would attribute one constant value for both the energy needs and GHG emissions to litter management despite differences in impact intensities. Capturing the specific environmental impact of the litter is difficult when those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. Finally, economic allocation assumes that poultry litter is always a co-product of poultry production with an economic value; but, depending on geographic and temporal

factors the same litter could be considered a by-product or even a waste with little to no economic value. In Maryland, poultry litter is unevenly distributed geographically and the co-product (i.e. poultry litter) can have fertilizer value that exceeds handling costs in one location while the handling costs are greater than the fertilizer value in another lo- cation. Such factors make it difficult to assign one constant dollar value to poultry litter, even within a single state.

The International Standards Organization (ISO) provides re- commendations on how to allocate co-products. ISO recommends system expansion whenever possible to avoid the need for co-product allocation. System expansion involves expanding the boundaries of an assessment to include indirect impacts of a production system outside the scope of the system in question (ISO, 1998). Using this method, poultry farms would be credited with the environmental benefit of avoided synthetic fertilizer production through the production of poultry litter for fertilizing crops. When system expansion is not rea- listic and allocation of environmental impacts cannot be avoided, the ISO recommends that allocation reflect physical relationships between a process and its environmental impacts, or some other relationship between products and co-products. Economic allocation is reserved for when other allocation methods are not possible or realistic (ISO, 1998).

The mass-energy approach allocates environmental burdens from litter based on a biophysical relationship between poultry litter and the inputs needed for its production. These inputs include industrially produced or processed fertilizers that were applied in conventional cropping systems to grow feed for poultry. Most of the nutrients found in poultry litter, therefore, originated from conventional production systems. This mass-energy methodology follows the ISO 14044 re- commendation that the "inventory is based on material balances be- tween inputs and outputs. Allocation procedures should therefore ap- proximate as much as possible such fundamental input/output relationships and characteristics" (ISO, 2006). Such a method is useful when quantifying the dependence of organic farming systems on con- ventional production processes since the use of litter is tied directly to the production of nutrients in the litter.

In this paper, we present two allocation methods. We used economic allocation, which assigns values for energy consumption and GHG emissions from poultry litter production based on the economic value of poultry litter, since it has been the most common allocation method used in previous livestock life cycle analyses (LCAs) and therefore al- lows our results to be compared with previous studies (Thomassen et al., 2008). This method is also appealing since poultry are raised for their meat or eggs, and litter is a co-product that would be produced whether or not it is applied to farms. Therefore, it can be argued that litter should only be charged a proportion of the environmental impacts from poultry production based on the relatively small economic value compared to poultry meat.

Recognizing the limits of economic allocation mentioned above, we also conducted allocation on a mass-energy basis. This method reflects the flow of biologically valuable resources (N, P, and K) and their en- vironmental impacts. The benefit of this methodology is that it directly reflects the relationship between poultry litter and the resources needed to produce that litter. Feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental impact of poultry production (Boggia et al., 2010), and this feed is typically grown in conventional cropping systems that de- pend on synthetic fertilizer, synthetic herbicides and pesticides. Taking into account the inputs needed to produce poultry litter and the nu- trients found in the litter may better reflect the true environmental costs of using poultry litter in both organic and conventional cropping sys- tems.

We chose not to use system expansion since our goal was to allocate the cost of manure nutrients to the organic systems to recognize their dependence on synthetic nutrients and the associated environmental impacts. Additionally, due to the uneven geographic distribution of poultry litter in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, transportation of poultry litter from areas of production to farms across the state is often

subsidized to limit nutrient runoff into the Chesapeake Bay (http:// ida.maryland.gov/resource_conservation/Pages/manure_ management.aspx https://dda.delaware.gov/nutrients/nm_reloc. shtml). System expansion would credit the poultry farm for displacing the production of synthetic fertilizer but would ignore the fact that excess poultry litter carries other environmental burdens. If these nu- trients are not recycled as fertilizer they can end up in the Chesapeake Bay, which can contribute to harmful algal blooms and other environ- mental problems. Given this situation, it is important to track the en- vironmental burden of the specific nutrients in the poultry litter rather than assuming poultry production in general produces only environ- mental benefits through synthetic fertilizer displacement in the mar- ketplace.

The primary goal of this study was to quantify GHG emissions and energy use in organic systems, including those associated with the nutrients that originated through industrial processes used in conven- tional agriculture. We conducted an analysis of energy use and GHG emissions in five different cropping systems in the FSP, a long-term grain cropping systems field experiment which contains both organic and conventional treatments (Cavigelli et al., 2008). We ultimately sought to identify which inputs and management decisions drove dif- ferences in energy use and GHG emissions. The scope of our analysis was limited to the farm gate.
14: The FSP, which is described in detail in Cavigelli et al. (2008), is a long-term cropping systems trial established in 1996 to compare the agronomic, economic, and environmental sustainability of organic and conventional grain crop production. The FSP includes five different cropping systems (Table Conventional no-till (NT) and conventional chisel-till (CT) systems both follow the same three-year rotation of corn soybean - wheat/soybean double cropped (DC). The three organic systems include a two-year rotation of corn-soybean (Org2), a three- year rotation of corn-soybean-wheat (Org3), and a six-year rotation of corn-soybean-wheat-alfalfa (Org6). The corn crop was followed by a cereal rye (Secale cereal L.) cover crop in all five systems. In both Org2 and Org3 a hairy vetch (Vicia villosa Roth) cover crop was planted the fall prior to spring corn planting. The hairy vetch was incorporated by disk and moldboard plow in the spring to supply N to the corn crop. All organic systems were tilled, although Org6 was not tilled during the three years that alfalfa was present. Poultry litter was applied to sup- plement vetch or alfalfa crops during the corn phase of each organic rotation when legume biomass production was not sufficient to meet corn N needs. Poultry litter also served as the sole source of added P in all systems and all nutrients for organic wheat crops (Org3 and Org6). For each of the five systems, all crop rotation entry points were present every year.
We present energy use and GHG emissions on both per area (ha) and per crop yield bases. The per area basis functional unit was calculated

by multiplying energy or emissions by area represented by individual crops within the rotation for a given year and then summing across crops. Presenting results on a per area basis allows for comparison of different crop rotations. We also present data on a per crop yield basis to account for differences in yields among systems. We summed yields of crops within each crop rotation to reflect the frequency of each crop in the rotation (e.g. alfalfa yield was multiplied by three to reflect that it is present three of six years in the Org6 rotation). Energy use and GHG emissions attributed to rye and legume cover crops were allocated to soybean and corn, respectively, in this yield analysis. Corn and soybean, but not winter wheat, yields were lower in FSP organic than conven- tional systems (Tables A2-A6; Teasdale and Cavigelli, 2017; Cavigelli et al., 2008).

We used the Farm Energy Analysis Tool (FEAT), an open-source database model for quantifying energy use and GHG emissions of cropping systems (available at www.ecologicalmodels.psu.edu/ agroecology/feat). Camargo et al. (2013) provide a full description of the FEAT model, including the methods used to calculate total energy use and GHG emissions for each input. We parameterized the model with FEAT database default values and actual inputs and crop yields for each FSP cropping system, based on average values for the years 2003 through 2013. While the FSP plots were established in 1996, current management practices were fully adopted in 2003.

The boundary of this analysis was limited to the farm gate (Fig. 1). We assumed no on-farm processing took place besides grain drying, and that all crops were sold as commodities. Primary and secondary sources of inputs included in this analysis were on-farm fuel use, labor, trans- portation of inputs to the farm, and the production of those inputs in- cluding N, P and K fertilizers, poultry litter, lime, seed, and herbicides. The parameters evaluated were energy use (MJ ha 1 year and MJ kg dry matter year GHG emissions (kg CO2e ha kg COe kg dry matter year and crop production (Mg We analyzed all GHG emissions, but because FEAT does not account for changes in soil C that would reflect net ecosystem exchange of CO2 (Robertson and Grace, 2004) we did not include changes in soil C.

We first used economic allocation to allow comparisons with other studies in the literature. In Maryland, poultry litter accounts for on average 1.65% of a poultry operation's annual profits (Rhodes and Timmons, 2011), SO our economic allocation method assigned this percentage of the environmental impacts from poultry production, based on values found in Pelletier (2008), to the litter applied to the organic cropping systems.

We then developed a methodology to track the reliance of organic cropping systems on conventional poultry production systems for poultry litter as a source of fertilizer nutrients using a mass-energy al- location method. Since our interest was in quantifying the environ- mental impact of all inputs used in the FSP, it was necessary to account for energy use and GHG emissions from the original production of nutrients found in poultry litter through synthetic fertilizer production (N) and nutrient mining (P and K). The FSP organic systems were

In Maryland, poultry litter accounts for on average 1.65% of a poultry operation's annual profits (Rhodes and Timmons, 2011), SO our economic allocation method assigned this percentage of the environmental impacts from poultry production, based on values found in Pelletier (2008), to the litter applied to the organic cropping systems.

We then developed a methodology to track the reliance of organic cropping systems on conventional poultry production systems for poultry litter as a source of fertilizer nutrients using a mass-energy al- location method. Since our interest was in quantifying the environ- mental impact of all inputs used in the FSP, it was necessary to account for energy use and GHG emissions from the original production of nutrients found in poultry litter through synthetic fertilizer production (N) and nutrient mining (P and K). 17: The FSP, which is described in detail in Cavigelli et al. (2008), is a long-term cropping systems trial established in 1996 to compare the agronomic, economic, and environmental sustainability of organic and conventional grain crop production. The FSP includes five different cropping systems (Table Conventional no-till (NT) and conventional chisel-till (CT) systems both follow the same three-year rotation of corn soybean - wheat/soybean double cropped (DC). The three organic systems include a two-year rotation of corn-soybean (Org2), a three- year rotation of corn-soybean-wheat (Org3), and a six-year rotation of corn-soybean-wheat-alfalfa (Org6). The corn crop was followed by a cereal rye (Secale cereal L.) cover crop in all five systems. In both Org2 and Org3 a hairy vetch (Vicia villosa Roth) cover crop was planted the fall prior to spring corn planting. The hairy vetch was incorporated by disk and moldboard plow in the spring to supply N to the corn crop. All organic systems were tilled, although Org6 was not tilled during the three years that alfalfa was present. Poultry litter was applied to sup- plement vetch or alfalfa crops during the corn phase of each organic rotation when legume biomass production was not sufficient to meet corn N needs. Poultry litter also served as the sole source of added P in all systems and all nutrients for organic wheat crops (Org3 and Org6). For each of the five systems, all crop rotation entry points were present every year.
We present energy use and GHG emissions on both per area (ha) and per crop yield bases. The per area basis functional unit was calculated

by multiplying energy or emissions by area represented by individual crops within the rotation for a given year and then summing across crops. Presenting results on a per area basis allows for comparison of different crop rotations. We also present data on a per crop yield basis to account for differences in yields among systems. We summed yields of crops within each crop rotation to reflect the frequency of each crop in the rotation (e.g. alfalfa yield was multiplied by three to reflect that it is present three of six years in the Org6 rotation). Energy use and GHG emissions attributed to rye and legume cover crops were allocated to soybean and corn, respectively, in this yield analysis. Corn and soybean, but not winter wheat, yields were lower in FSP organic than conven- tional systems (Tables A2-A6; Teasdale and Cavigelli, 2017; Cavigelli et al., 2008).

We used the Farm Energy Analysis Tool (FEAT), an open-source database model for quantifying energy use and GHG emissions of cropping systems (available at www.ecologicalmodels.psu.edu/ agroecology/feat). Camargo et al. (2013) provide a full description of the FEAT model, including the methods used to calculate total energy use and GHG emissions for each input. We parameterized the model with FEAT database default values and actual inputs and crop yields for each FSP cropping system, based on average values for the years 2003 through 2013. While the FSP plots were established in 1996, current management practices were fully adopted in 2003.

The boundary of this analysis was limited to the farm gate (Fig. 1). We assumed no on-farm processing took place besides grain drying, and that all crops were sold as commodities. Primary and secondary sources of inputs included in this analysis were on-farm fuel use, labor, trans- portation of inputs to the farm, and the production of those inputs in- cluding N, P and K fertilizers, poultry litter, lime, seed, and herbicides. The parameters evaluated were energy use (MJ ha 1 year and MJ kg dry matter year GHG emissions (kg CO2e ha kg COe kg dry matter year and crop production (Mg We analyzed all GHG emissions, but because FEAT does not account for changes in soil C that would reflect net ecosystem exchange of CO2 (Robertson and Grace, 2004) we did not include changes in soil C.

We first used economic allocation to allow comparisons with other studies in the literature. In Maryland, poultry litter accounts for on average 1.65% of a poultry operation's annual profits (Rhodes and Timmons, 2011), SO our economic allocation method assigned this percentage of the environmental impacts from poultry production, based on values found in Pelletier (2008), to the litter applied to the organic cropping systems.

We then developed a methodology to track the reliance of organic cropping systems on conventional poultry production systems for poultry litter as a source of fertilizer nutrients using a mass-energy al- location method. Since our interest was in quantifying the environ- mental impact of all inputs used in the FSP, it was necessary to account for energy use and GHG emissions from the original production of nutrients found in poultry litter through synthetic fertilizer production (N) and nutrient mining (P and K). charged for the production of N, P, and K that was imported in the form of poultry litter. We assumed that conventional corn fed to chickens received 48% of its N from synthetic fertilizer and the remaining N from soybean in rotation (13%) or from soil N reserves (39%) (Hesterman et al., 1987). Nitrogen taken up by the corn was used as a surrogate for in season N application since N uptake is very similar to application rate. We also assumed that conventional soybeans in the feed fixed their own N, and that 100% of the P and K originated from mining opera- tions. For both allocation methods, we assumed poultry were fed a simplified diet of 67% corn and 33% soybeans (adjusted mass balance- based feed formulation based on Longo et al. (2004) and Meng and Slominski (2005)). For this mass-energy allocation method, the energy and GHG emissions required to produce poultry litter are the sum of the energy (or GHG emissions) to produce 100% of the P and K applied, and 32% (67% X 48%) of the litter N applied. We assumed poultry litter was transported 15 km from the poultry house to the organic farm (see Table A1 for sample calculation).

Inputs and crop yields for each cropping system, based on average values from FSP for the years 2003 to 2013, are presented in Tables A2-A6. Each cropping system was assumed to have 400 ha under production each year. Other values used in the model include herbicide application rates in the NT and CT systems (Table A7); fuel use and labor requirements for individual operations in NT and CT systems (Table A8) and in the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems (Table A9). Energy values for the production of seed coatings are reported in Ryan (2010).

We assumed poultry litter was transported 15 km from the poultry house to the organic farm (see Table A1 for sample calculation).

Inputs and crop yields for each cropping system, based on average values from FSP for the years 2003 to 2013, are presented in Tables A2-A6. Each cropping system was assumed to have 400 ha under production each year. Other values used in the model include herbicide application rates in the NT and CT systems (Table A7); fuel use and labor requirements for individual operations in NT and CT systems (Table A8) and in the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems (Table A9). Energy values for the production of seed coatings are reported in Ryan (2010).
24: Energy use for the full rotation in all three organic systems was less than in the two conventional systems on an area basis (Table 2; Fig. 2a). On a per crop yield basis, however, energy use was greater in Org2 than in CT, NT and Org3, and lowest in Org6 (Fig. 2c). Greenhouse gas emissions, on the other hand, were highest in the Org2 and Org3 sys- items, intermediate in NT and CT, and lowest in Org6 whether expressed on per area or yield bases (Table 2 and Fig. 2b, d). Energy use and GHG emissions values were greater when mass-energy rather than economic allocation was used for poultry litter values whether expressed on per

area or yield bases. On a per area basis, energy use was 15%, 15%, and 9% greater and GHG emissions were 3.8%, 3.4%, and 2.3% greater using mass-energy rather than economic allocation for the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems, respectively (Table 2).

The largest contributors to energy use in all systems were fertilizer production (either as synthetic fertilizer or poultry litter) and on-farm diesel fuel (Table 2). On a per area basis fertilizer production (N, P, and K) in the NT and CT systems accounted for 46% and 45% of total energy use (MJ ha 1 year respectively. Nitrogen production accounted for 86% of the energy to produce N, P, and K fertilizers used in NT and CT. Diesel fuel accounted for 16% and 25% of the total energy use of the NT and CT systems, respectively.

Diesel fuel in the organic systems accounted for 42% of total energy use in the Org2 and Org3 systems and 44% in the Org6 system on a per area basis. Fertilizer production and transport (poultry litter production and transport, and K2O production and application) accounted for 27% to 29% of the total energy use for the three organic systems.

The largest contributors to GHG emissions in CT and NT systems was associated with fertilizer production and application (Table 2). On a per area basis, GHG emissions from fertilizer production (N, P, and K) and N2O emissions from fertilizer applied to soil accounted for 54% and 55% of the total GHG emissions (kg COe ha 1 year from the NT and CT systems, respectively. On a per area basis, the largest con- tributor to GHG emissions in the organic systems was N2O emissions from crop residues, accounting for 31-35% of total GHG emissions. Poultry litter production and N2O emissions from litter applied to soil accounted for 27%, 29%, and 23% of total GHG emissions from the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems, respectively.

Fig. 2 illustrates how different crops in each system contributed to total energy and GHG emission levels in an average year. For example, an average hectare of corn used more energy and released higher levels of GHG emissions than any other crop in NT, CT and Org2 while corn and wheat accounted for similar levels of energy use and GHG emis- sions in Org3 and Org6. Alfalfa, which was present on half of the acreage in Org6, accounted for the bulk of energy use and GHG emis- sions in Org6 despite overall energy and GHG emissions being lowest in Org6 than any of the other systems. 33: Our study characterizes the energy use and GHG emissions from five cropping systems at the USDA FSP in Beltsville, Maryland, highlighting differences between conventional and organic farm management practices and among organic crop rotations at the site using the FEAT model. To the best of our knowledge, this paper represents the first attempt to quantify the energy use and GHG emissions from nutrients used in organic systems that originated through industrial processes used in conventional agriculture.

The largest contributor to energy use in the NT and CT systems, on a per area basis, was the production of synthetic fertilizer (primarily N) followed by on-farm diesel fuel use for traction. Diesel fuel accounted for the largest share of energy use in the organic systems, followed by poultry litter production, regardless of how the energy for poultry litter production was allocated (Table 2). One limitation of this study was the assumption that poultry litter would be transported 15 km; energy use would be slightly lower if the litter were produced on-farm, and greater the longer the distance between the production and the application sites. Greenhouse gas emissions from the NT and CT systems were greatest for N2O emissions from N fertilizer applied to soil, followed by CO2 emissions from N fertilizer production and N2O emissions from crop residues. The largest percentage of GHG emissions in the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems was N2O emissions from crop residues, fol- lowed by N2O emissions from poultry litter field application, followed by on-farm diesel use (Table 2). Since FEAT uses the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Tier 1 method to predict N2O emis- sions, substituting measured values or improved models could be used to further assess the accuracy of the FEAT model. Even so, the ordinal results using the Tier 1 method are supported by preliminary published values of N2O emissions from FSP in which the Org2 and Org 3 systems emit higher levels of N2O than Org6, CT and NT (Cavigelli and Parkin, 2012).

The organic systems used less energy than the conventional systems on a per area basis. This difference was driven largely by the fact that synthetic N fertilizer production through the Haber-Bosch process is energy-intensive, and contributed 45-46% of the total energy use (MJ and about 20% of the total GHG emissions (kg COe ha year-1 in the NT and CT systems (Table 2). Due to lower crop yields in the organic systems, however, energy use per unit crop production showed a different pattern, being greatest in Org2, inter- mediate in CT, NT, and Org3 and lowest in Org6 (Fig. 2).

Interestingly, the Org2 and Org3 systems had the highest levels of GHG emissions, whether expressed on a per area or crop yield basis, despite these systems using less energy than the NT and CT systems. Differences were largely driven by higher levels of N2O emissions from both poultry litter and crop residue in Org2 and Org3 (Table 2). Our results for total energy and GHG emissions were similar to other pub- lished values (Table 3; Pimentel et al., 2005; Farrell et al., 2006; Pradhan et al., 2008; Camargo et al., 2013). Previous results from the FSP found that Org3 had a lower global warming potential than the NT and CT systems when expressed on a per area or per crop yield basis (Cavigelli et al., 2009) due primarily to higher levels of soil carbon (C) in Org3 than the CT and NT systems. While the 2009 study included net exchanges of CO2 with the soil, initial soil C levels were estimated, not measured. Net exchange of C or soil C sequestration is beyond the scope of our current study since FEAT does not account for this aspect of GHG emissions. Nonetheless, results from the FEAT analysis are consistent with the 2009 study, showing the NT and CT systems used more energy on an area basis than Org3 but that Org3 had higher levels of GHG emissions than NT and CT (Table 2). Organic systems in general have greater SOC than conventional tilled systems (Gattinger et al., 2012; Cavigelli et al., 2013), and understanding the net impact of having greater emissions (as in Org2 and Org3) and greater SOC accumulation in organic than conventional systems is important for determining management impacts on global warming potential. Not including SOC

Results from the FEAT model illustrate how crop rotation can in- fluence on-farm energy use and GHG emissions. Among organic

systems, energy use, whether expressed on a per area or per crop yield basis, was reduced as crop rotation length and complexity increased. The addition of winter wheat (Org3) to a corn-soybean (Org2) rotation reduced energy use by 6.2% and reduced GHG emissions by 2.7% on an area basis. (Org6) led to further reductions in energy use (14%) and GHG emis- sions (29%) compared to Org3. These results were largely driven by the amount of land dedicated to each crop. For example, half of the land in Org6 is dedicated to alfalfa, which requires fewer inputs, and therefore consumes less energy and emits fewer GHGs, than the NT, CT, Org2 and Org3 systems that are based solely on annual crops.

Previous studies have also found that including perennial crops in agricultural systems can reduce energy use and GHG emissions while also increasing productivity (Davis et al., 2012). Perennial crops can reduce GHG emissions through reduced tillage requirements and soil erosion and increased carbon sequestration (Pimentel et al., 2012). Perennial crops offer additional environmental benefits including higher nutrient retention, less fertilizer and herbicide use, and fewer tractor operations (Glover et al., 2010). Our results are consistent with Malcolm et al. (2015), showing that crop production systems that in- tegrate annual and perennial crops have lower energy use and GHG emissions than those that include only annual crops.

Mass-energy allocation ties the specific nutrients found in poultry litter to the energy and GHG emissions needed for their production. This accounts for the partial dependence of organic cropping systems on conventional systems for nutrients. While organic farms in the US do not use synthetic fertilizers, much of the N, P, and K found in poultry litter originated from industrial processes. As described, we assumed 32% of the N in poultry litter originated from the Haber-Bosch process, with the remainder originating from natural N fixation and soil N. We also assumed that 100% of the P and K originated from industrially mined sources. Specific values of nutrient dependence will vary across crops and locations, but these assumptions are in line with the limited research available on the reliance of organic farming on conventional systems. Nowak et al. (2013) determined that organic farms in France relied on conventional livestock operations for 23%, 73%, and 53% of their N, P, and K, respectively. Ringeval et al. (2014) found that, on average, 82% of the P found in all agricultural soils originated from mined phosphate rock.

A review of LCAs on livestock production found that economic al- location was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as litter (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its popu- larity in the literature, this method has been the subject of recent cri- ticism as noted in the Introduction. It is challenging to capture the environmental impacts of litter if those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. More research is needed to further develop the mass-energy method of allocation for poultry litter, in particular the development of a more complex model to differentiate between synthetically-produced versus biologically-produced nutrients in litter based on more realistic poultry feed formulations. Additionally, N fixation by soybean in the various systems is likely to differ (Green et al., unpublished data), which could also influence results. A similar methodology should be developed for other livestock-based products, such as dairy manure, which is often used for fertilizer in organic cropping systems.

We illustrate how energy use and GHG emissions can be reduced if N inputs, in the form of either synthetic fertilizer or poultry litter, and on-farm diesel use are reduced. Further research is needed to increase nitrogen use efficiency (NUE), especially for animal manures, which have larger residual impacts on soil C and N reserves than do fertilizers (Cavigelli and Dao, 2008). Increasing NUE will also reduce the risk of nutrient runoff and nitrate leaching, which are of particular concern to farms in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, where the FSP is located.

(2013)|16,330|-|7273|-|2542 -|-|-|- Pradhan et al. (2008)|-|-|4032-15,506|-|2441-4201|-|-|- Farrell et al. (2006)|5728-12,066|-|-|-||-|-|- Pimentel et al. (2005)|21,757|15,481|8786|9623|-|||
