(Org6) led to further reductions in energy use (14%) and GHG emis- sions (29%) compared to Org3. These results were largely driven by the amount of land dedicated to each crop. For example, half of the land in Org6 is dedicated to alfalfa, which requires fewer inputs, and therefore consumes less energy and emits fewer GHGs, than the NT, CT, Org2 and Org3 systems that are based solely on annual crops.

Previous studies have also found that including perennial crops in agricultural systems can reduce energy use and GHG emissions while also increasing productivity (Davis et al., 2012). Perennial crops can reduce GHG emissions through reduced tillage requirements and soil erosion and increased carbon sequestration (Pimentel et al., 2012). Perennial crops offer additional environmental benefits including higher nutrient retention, less fertilizer and herbicide use, and fewer tractor operations (Glover et al., 2010). Our results are consistent with Malcolm et al. (2015), showing that crop production systems that in- tegrate annual and perennial crops have lower energy use and GHG emissions than those that include only annual crops.

Mass-energy allocation ties the specific nutrients found in poultry litter to the energy and GHG emissions needed for their production. This accounts for the partial dependence of organic cropping systems on conventional systems for nutrients. While organic farms in the US do not use synthetic fertilizers, much of the N, P, and K found in poultry litter originated from industrial processes. As described, we assumed 32% of the N in poultry litter originated from the Haber-Bosch process, with the remainder originating from natural N fixation and soil N. We also assumed that 100% of the P and K originated from industrially mined sources. Specific values of nutrient dependence will vary across crops and locations, but these assumptions are in line with the limited research available on the reliance of organic farming on conventional systems. Nowak et al. (2013) determined that organic farms in France relied on conventional livestock operations for 23%, 73%, and 53% of their N, P, and K, respectively. Ringeval et al. (2014) found that, on average, 82% of the P found in all agricultural soils originated from mined phosphate rock.

A review of LCAs on livestock production found that economic al- location was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as litter (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its popu- larity in the literature, this method has been the subject of recent cri- ticism as noted in the Introduction. It is challenging to capture the environmental impacts of litter if those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. More research is needed to further develop the mass-energy method of allocation for poultry litter, in particular the development of a more complex model to differentiate between synthetically-produced versus biologically-produced nutrients in litter based on more realistic poultry feed formulations. Additionally, N fixation by soybean in the various systems is likely to differ (Green et al., unpublished data), which could also influence results. A similar methodology should be developed for other livestock-based products, such as dairy manure, which is often used for fertilizer in organic cropping systems.

We illustrate how energy use and GHG emissions can be reduced if N inputs, in the form of either synthetic fertilizer or poultry litter, and on-farm diesel use are reduced. Further research is needed to increase nitrogen use efficiency (NUE), especially for animal manures, which have larger residual impacts on soil C and N reserves than do fertilizers (Cavigelli and Dao, 2008). Increasing NUE will also reduce the risk of nutrient runoff and nitrate leaching, which are of particular concern to farms in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, where the FSP is located.
36: Our study characterizes the energy use and GHG emissions from five cropping systems at the USDA FSP in Beltsville, Maryland, highlighting differences between conventional and organic farm management practices and among organic crop rotations at the site using the FEAT model. To the best of our knowledge, this paper represents the first attempt to quantify the energy use and GHG emissions from nutrients used in organic systems that originated through industrial processes used in conventional agriculture.

The largest contributor to energy use in the NT and CT systems, on a per area basis, was the production of synthetic fertilizer (primarily N) followed by on-farm diesel fuel use for traction. Diesel fuel accounted for the largest share of energy use in the organic systems, followed by poultry litter production, regardless of how the energy for poultry litter production was allocated (Table 2). One limitation of this study was the assumption that poultry litter would be transported 15 km; energy use would be slightly lower if the litter were produced on-farm, and greater the longer the distance between the production and the application sites. Greenhouse gas emissions from the NT and CT systems were greatest for N2O emissions from N fertilizer applied to soil, followed by CO2 emissions from N fertilizer production and N2O emissions from crop residues. The largest percentage of GHG emissions in the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems was N2O emissions from crop residues, fol- lowed by N2O emissions from poultry litter field application, followed by on-farm diesel use (Table 2). Since FEAT uses the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Tier 1 method to predict N2O emis- sions, substituting measured values or improved models could be used to further assess the accuracy of the FEAT model. Even so, the ordinal results using the Tier 1 method are supported by preliminary published values of N2O emissions from FSP in which the Org2 and Org 3 systems emit higher levels of N2O than Org6, CT and NT (Cavigelli and Parkin, 2012).

The organic systems used less energy than the conventional systems on a per area basis. This difference was driven largely by the fact that synthetic N fertilizer production through the Haber-Bosch process is energy-intensive, and contributed 45-46% of the total energy use (MJ and about 20% of the total GHG emissions (kg COe ha year-1 in the NT and CT systems (Table 2). Due to lower crop yields in the organic systems, however, energy use per unit crop production showed a different pattern, being greatest in Org2, inter- mediate in CT, NT, and Org3 and lowest in Org6 (Fig. 2).

Interestingly, the Org2 and Org3 systems had the highest levels of GHG emissions, whether expressed on a per area or crop yield basis, despite these systems using less energy than the NT and CT systems. Differences were largely driven by higher levels of N2O emissions from both poultry litter and crop residue in Org2 and Org3 (Table 2). Our results for total energy and GHG emissions were similar to other pub- lished values (Table 3; Pimentel et al., 2005; Farrell et al., 2006; Pradhan et al., 2008; Camargo et al., 2013). Previous results from the FSP found that Org3 had a lower global warming potential than the NT and CT systems when expressed on a per area or per crop yield basis (Cavigelli et al., 2009) due primarily to higher levels of soil carbon (C) in Org3 than the CT and NT systems. While the 2009 study included net exchanges of CO2 with the soil, initial soil C levels were estimated, not measured. Net exchange of C or soil C sequestration is beyond the scope of our current study since FEAT does not account for this aspect of GHG emissions. Nonetheless, results from the FEAT analysis are consistent with the 2009 study, showing the NT and CT systems used more energy on an area basis than Org3 but that Org3 had higher levels of GHG emissions than NT and CT (Table 2). Organic systems in general have greater SOC than conventional tilled systems (Gattinger et al., 2012; Cavigelli et al., 2013), and understanding the net impact of having greater emissions (as in Org2 and Org3) and greater SOC accumulation in organic than conventional systems is important for determining management impacts on global warming potential. A review of LCAs on livestock production found that economic al- location was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as litter (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its popu- larity in the literature, this method has been the subject of recent cri- ticism as noted in the Introduction. It is challenging to capture the environmental impacts of litter if those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. More research is needed to further develop the mass-energy method of allocation for poultry litter, in particular the development of a more complex model to differentiate between synthetically-produced versus biologically-produced nutrients in litter based on more realistic poultry feed formulations. Additionally, N fixation by soybean in the various systems is likely to differ (Green et al., unpublished data), which could also influence results. A similar methodology should be developed for other livestock-based products, such as dairy manure, which is often used for fertilizer in organic cropping systems.

We illustrate how energy use and GHG emissions can be reduced if N inputs, in the form of either synthetic fertilizer or poultry litter, and on-farm diesel use are reduced. Further research is needed to increase nitrogen use efficiency (NUE), especially for animal manures, which have larger residual impacts on soil C and N reserves than do fertilizers (Cavigelli and Dao, 2008). Increasing NUE will also reduce the risk of nutrient runoff and nitrate leaching, which are of particular concern to farms in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, where the FSP is located.
39: Our study characterizes the energy use and GHG emissions from five cropping systems at the USDA FSP in Beltsville, Maryland, highlighting differences between conventional and organic farm management practices and among organic crop rotations at the site using the FEAT model. To the best of our knowledge, this paper represents the first attempt to quantify the energy use and GHG emissions from nutrients used in organic systems that originated through industrial processes used in conventional agriculture.

The largest contributor to energy use in the NT and CT systems, on a per area basis, was the production of synthetic fertilizer (primarily N) followed by on-farm diesel fuel use for traction. Diesel fuel accounted for the largest share of energy use in the organic systems, followed by poultry litter production, regardless of how the energy for poultry litter production was allocated (Table 2). One limitation of this study was the assumption that poultry litter would be transported 15 km; energy use would be slightly lower if the litter were produced on-farm, and greater the longer the distance between the production and the application sites. Greenhouse gas emissions from the NT and CT systems were greatest for N2O emissions from N fertilizer applied to soil, followed by CO2 emissions from N fertilizer production and N2O emissions from crop residues. The largest percentage of GHG emissions in the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems was N2O emissions from crop residues, fol- lowed by N2O emissions from poultry litter field application, followed by on-farm diesel use (Table 2). Since FEAT uses the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Tier 1 method to predict N2O emis- sions, substituting measured values or improved models could be used to further assess the accuracy of the FEAT model. Even so, the ordinal results using the Tier 1 method are supported by preliminary published values of N2O emissions from FSP in which the Org2 and Org 3 systems emit higher levels of N2O than Org6, CT and NT (Cavigelli and Parkin, 2012).

The organic systems used less energy than the conventional systems on a per area basis. This difference was driven largely by the fact that synthetic N fertilizer production through the Haber-Bosch process is energy-intensive, and contributed 45-46% of the total energy use (MJ and about 20% of the total GHG emissions (kg COe ha year-1 in the NT and CT systems (Table 2). Due to lower crop yields in the organic systems, however, energy use per unit crop production showed a different pattern, being greatest in Org2, inter- mediate in CT, NT, and Org3 and lowest in Org6 (Fig. 2).

Interestingly, the Org2 and Org3 systems had the highest levels of GHG emissions, whether expressed on a per area or crop yield basis, despite these systems using less energy than the NT and CT systems. Differences were largely driven by higher levels of N2O emissions from both poultry litter and crop residue in Org2 and Org3 (Table 2). Our results for total energy and GHG emissions were similar to other pub- lished values (Table 3; Pimentel et al., 2005; Farrell et al., 2006; Pradhan et al., 2008; Camargo et al., 2013). Previous results from the FSP found that Org3 had a lower global warming potential than the NT and CT systems when expressed on a per area or per crop yield basis (Cavigelli et al., 2009) due primarily to higher levels of soil carbon (C) in Org3 than the CT and NT systems. While the 2009 study included net exchanges of CO2 with the soil, initial soil C levels were estimated, not measured. Net exchange of C or soil C sequestration is beyond the scope of our current study since FEAT does not account for this aspect of GHG emissions. Nonetheless, results from the FEAT analysis are consistent with the 2009 study, showing the NT and CT systems used more energy on an area basis than Org3 but that Org3 had higher levels of GHG emissions than NT and CT (Table 2). Organic systems in general have greater SOC than conventional tilled systems (Gattinger et al., 2012; Cavigelli et al., 2013), and understanding the net impact of having greater emissions (as in Org2 and Org3) and greater SOC accumulation in organic than conventional systems is important for determining management impacts on global warming potential. The organic systems used less energy than the conventional systems on a per area basis. This difference was driven largely by the fact that synthetic N fertilizer production through the Haber-Bosch process is energy-intensive, and contributed 45-46% of the total energy use (MJ and about 20% of the total GHG emissions (kg COe ha year-1 in the NT and CT systems (Table 2). Due to lower crop yields in the organic systems, however, energy use per unit crop production showed a different pattern, being greatest in Org2, inter- mediate in CT, NT, and Org3 and lowest in Org6 (Fig. 2).

Interestingly, the Org2 and Org3 systems had the highest levels of GHG emissions, whether expressed on a per area or crop yield basis, despite these systems using less energy than the NT and CT systems. Differences were largely driven by higher levels of N2O emissions from both poultry litter and crop residue in Org2 and Org3 (Table 2). Our results for total energy and GHG emissions were similar to other pub- lished values (Table 3; Pimentel et al., 2005; Farrell et al., 2006; Pradhan et al., 2008; Camargo et al., 2013). Previous results from the FSP found that Org3 had a lower global warming potential than the NT and CT systems when expressed on a per area or per crop yield basis (Cavigelli et al., 2009) due primarily to higher levels of soil carbon (C) in Org3 than the CT and NT systems. While the 2009 study included net exchanges of CO2 with the soil, initial soil C levels were estimated, not measured. Net exchange of C or soil C sequestration is beyond the scope of our current study since FEAT does not account for this aspect of GHG emissions. Nonetheless, results from the FEAT analysis are consistent with the 2009 study, showing the NT and CT systems used more energy on an area basis than Org3 but that Org3 had higher levels of GHG emissions than NT and CT (Table 2). Organic systems in general have greater SOC than conventional tilled systems (Gattinger et al., 2012; Cavigelli et al., 2013), and understanding the net impact of having greater emissions (as in Org2 and Org3) and greater SOC accumulation in organic than conventional systems is important for determining management impacts on global warming potential. (Org6) led to further reductions in energy use (14%) and GHG emis- sions (29%) compared to Org3. These results were largely driven by the amount of land dedicated to each crop. For example, half of the land in Org6 is dedicated to alfalfa, which requires fewer inputs, and therefore consumes less energy and emits fewer GHGs, than the NT, CT, Org2 and Org3 systems that are based solely on annual crops.

Previous studies have also found that including perennial crops in agricultural systems can reduce energy use and GHG emissions while also increasing productivity (Davis et al., 2012). Perennial crops can reduce GHG emissions through reduced tillage requirements and soil erosion and increased carbon sequestration (Pimentel et al., 2012). Perennial crops offer additional environmental benefits including higher nutrient retention, less fertilizer and herbicide use, and fewer tractor operations (Glover et al., 2010). Our results are consistent with Malcolm et al. (2015), showing that crop production systems that in- tegrate annual and perennial crops have lower energy use and GHG emissions than those that include only annual crops.

Mass-energy allocation ties the specific nutrients found in poultry litter to the energy and GHG emissions needed for their production. This accounts for the partial dependence of organic cropping systems on conventional systems for nutrients. While organic farms in the US do not use synthetic fertilizers, much of the N, P, and K found in poultry litter originated from industrial processes. As described, we assumed 32% of the N in poultry litter originated from the Haber-Bosch process, with the remainder originating from natural N fixation and soil N. We also assumed that 100% of the P and K originated from industrially mined sources. Specific values of nutrient dependence will vary across crops and locations, but these assumptions are in line with the limited research available on the reliance of organic farming on conventional systems. Nowak et al. (2013) determined that organic farms in France relied on conventional livestock operations for 23%, 73%, and 53% of their N, P, and K, respectively. Ringeval et al. (2014) found that, on average, 82% of the P found in all agricultural soils originated from mined phosphate rock.

A review of LCAs on livestock production found that economic al- location was the most commonly used method for handling animal co- products such as litter (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Despite its popu- larity in the literature, this method has been the subject of recent cri- ticism as noted in the Introduction. It is challenging to capture the environmental impacts of litter if those impacts fluctuate with market prices and other external economic forces. More research is needed to further develop the mass-energy method of allocation for poultry litter, in particular the development of a more complex model to differentiate between synthetically-produced versus biologically-produced nutrients in litter based on more realistic poultry feed formulations. Additionally, N fixation by soybean in the various systems is likely to differ (Green et al., unpublished data), which could also influence results. A similar methodology should be developed for other livestock-based products, such as dairy manure, which is often used for fertilizer in organic cropping systems.

We illustrate how energy use and GHG emissions can be reduced if N inputs, in the form of either synthetic fertilizer or poultry litter, and on-farm diesel use are reduced. Further research is needed to increase nitrogen use efficiency (NUE), especially for animal manures, which have larger residual impacts on soil C and N reserves than do fertilizers (Cavigelli and Dao, 2008). Increasing NUE will also reduce the risk of nutrient runoff and nitrate leaching, which are of particular concern to farms in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, where the FSP is located.
43: Our study characterizes the energy use and GHG emissions from five cropping systems at the USDA FSP in Beltsville, Maryland, highlighting differences between conventional and organic farm management practices and among organic crop rotations at the site using the FEAT model. To the best of our knowledge, this paper represents the first attempt to quantify the energy use and GHG emissions from nutrients used in organic systems that originated through industrial processes used in conventional agriculture.

The largest contributor to energy use in the NT and CT systems, on a per area basis, was the production of synthetic fertilizer (primarily N) followed by on-farm diesel fuel use for traction. Diesel fuel accounted for the largest share of energy use in the organic systems, followed by poultry litter production, regardless of how the energy for poultry litter production was allocated (Table 2). One limitation of this study was the assumption that poultry litter would be transported 15 km; energy use would be slightly lower if the litter were produced on-farm, and greater the longer the distance between the production and the application sites. Greenhouse gas emissions from the NT and CT systems were greatest for N2O emissions from N fertilizer applied to soil, followed by CO2 emissions from N fertilizer production and N2O emissions from crop residues. The largest percentage of GHG emissions in the Org2, Org3, and Org6 systems was N2O emissions from crop residues, fol- lowed by N2O emissions from poultry litter field application, followed by on-farm diesel use (Table 2). Since FEAT uses the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Tier 1 method to predict N2O emis- sions, substituting measured values or improved models could be used to further assess the accuracy of the FEAT model. Even so, the ordinal results using the Tier 1 method are supported by preliminary published values of N2O emissions from FSP in which the Org2 and Org 3 systems emit higher levels of N2O than Org6, CT and NT (Cavigelli and Parkin, 2012).

The organic systems used less energy than the conventional systems on a per area basis. This difference was driven largely by the fact that synthetic N fertilizer production through the Haber-Bosch process is energy-intensive, and contributed 45-46% of the total energy use (MJ and about 20% of the total GHG emissions (kg COe ha year-1 in the NT and CT systems (Table 2). Net exchange of C or soil C sequestration is beyond the scope of our current study since FEAT does not account for this aspect of GHG emissions. Nonetheless, results from the FEAT analysis are consistent with the 2009 study, showing the NT and CT systems used more energy on an area basis than Org3 but that Org3 had higher levels of GHG emissions than NT and CT (Table 2). 44: These results indicate that agricultural production dedicated largely to annual crops has a higher level of energy use and GHG emissions than systems that dedicate more land to perennial crops. Reducing the impact of farming on the environment can be achieved through man- agement practices that reduce inputs such as fertilizer and animal litter,
increase complexity of crop rotations and the use of perennial crops, decrease on-farm diesel use, and increase use of legume cover crops.

This study illustrates the importance of farm management in pro- moting sustainable food production. Organic practices are important in reducing the environmental impact of farming, particularly in reducing herbicide and pesticide use. However, organic farm management alone may not help to meet the global challenge of reducing GHG emissions. While reducing tillage and supplementing mineral fertilizer with le- gume cover crops are frequently cited as important strategies for im- proving the environmental sustainability of cropping systems, this study shows clearly that integrating perennials into annual grain crop production systems is more effective at reducing energy inputs and GHG emissions. Our results highlight the importance of understanding the factors that contribute to energy use and GHG emissions from agriculture, and demonstrate how such insights can be incorporated into farm management practices to minimize the impact of both con- ventional and organic farming on the environment.
